questions.question: Why is -log(fidelity) used instead of fidelity directly in Dijkstra's algorithm?

options.text: Converts multiplicative fidelity to additive cost for standard shortest path

questions.explanation: End-to-end fidelity is the product of link fidelities. Using -log converts this to a sum: -log(F1×F2) = -log(F1) + -log(F2). This allows standard shortest-path algorithms (which minimize sums) to maximize fidelity.

questions.question: In multi-metric optimization, what is a Pareto-optimal path?

options.text: A path where no other path is better in ALL metrics simultaneously

questions.explanation: A Pareto-optimal path is one where you cannot improve any metric without making another metric worse. The Pareto set contains all such non-dominated paths, giving the application choices that represent optimal trade-offs.

questions.question: What is the main challenge of using shortest hop count for quantum routing?

options.text: Fewer hops doesn't guarantee best fidelity or lowest latency

questions.explanation: In quantum networks, a longer path through high-fidelity links might have better end-to-end fidelity than a shorter path through low-fidelity links. Similarly, low-latency links might exist on longer paths.

questions.question: What does constrained path optimization do?

options.text: Finds any path regardless of quality
Optimizes primary metric while ensuring constraints are met

questions.explanation: Constrained optimization maximizes a primary metric (like fidelity) while ensuring other metrics stay within specified bounds (like latency < 50ms or hops < 5). Paths violating constraints are excluded.

questions.question: In a quantum routing protocol, what triggers routing table recomputation?

options.text: Changes in link state (fidelity, availability, new links)

questions.explanation: Routing tables are recomputed when link state changes - when links go up/down, when fidelity measurements change, or when new links are added. This keeps routes optimal for current network conditions.

questions.question: What is a key quantum-specific metric not found in classical routing?
questions.explanation: Entanglement generation rate is unique to quantum networks - it measures how many entangled pairs a link can produce per second. Classical networks don't have an equivalent concept.

questions.question: The optimal path for fidelity is always the same as the optimal path for latency.
questions.explanation: False. High-fidelity links may have higher latency (more careful operation), and low-latency links may have lower fidelity. The optimal path depends on which metric is prioritized, and they often differ.
